I have a question about a troubling possibility that has occurred to me about a video game, namely Undertale. The game is a deconstruction of RPG games, and ones of the endings is reached by murdering everyone in the game. This ending, called the genocide run, is truly difficult to achieve, due to the way that many of the character beg and plead for the lives; not just with the character, but with the player as well. There’s another weird thing which happens in this route, where your character mentions that the character's soul does not exist, and explicitly makes it clear it is the player’s soul driving things.

After killing everyone, the game world is destroyed, and the game displays a blank screen rather than any kind of menu. This remains even after resetting the game.

There is no way to replay it, as everything is gone; however, if you wait for ten minutes on the blank screen, the game will offer to restore the world in exchange for your soul. You have no character (since the entire world is gone, character included); and the game repeatedly breaks the fourth wall, and has some weird lines where your character says they don’t have a soul but the player does, all of which raises a very troubling question: when people accept the game’s offer to restore the world in exchange for their soul, what happens? Is it possible for people to actually accidentally sell their souls by doing this?

Given the game was released in September of 2015 and was an immediate success, shortly before things started getting really creepy and weird, it seems like it could explain a lot if even just a small fraction of the people who played it did in fact accidentally sell their souls.....

replies.content:
For what it's worth, this is treated as a bad thing in the game.

So the player-or-their-notional-proxy-or-something isn't making the exchange with "the game"; they're making an exchange with a character in the game -- the disembodied ghost of another human child from the backstory, a sociopathic one (having a close resemblance to the player character runaway child, which is used for a lot of narrative misdirection before the reveal; possibly the ghost is supposed to be a residue from the player character's past life). The ghost is ambiguously the narrator, but is not enough of a psychopath to actively push for wanton slaughter independently, in a normal playthrough just going with the flow narrating neutrally. However, in a genocide playthrough, the ghost is gradually strengthened in cruelty and in usurping the player-or-something's powers over the game world, as the player actively looks for fights with misguided spareable monsters to kill them and accumulate "EXP" and "LV", which are revealed in a twist to stand not for the usual "experience" and "levels", but for "EXecution Points" and "Level of Violence". (It's not direct genocide, it's just killing everything that can engage you in the battle interface where there's an option to kill it, and also a snowman monster. There's a shopkeeper monster who makes fun of the fact that you can't attack him through the shop interface, and how his holding you in conversation is giving the other monsters time to evacuate.) The ghost is the one who uses the player-or-whatever's power over the game world to nullify it entirely.

After agreeing to the exchange of their "SOUL" (after being harangued about thinking that they are above consequences), the player regains access to the main menu where they can make "full resets" and start a playthrough over. Normally a full reset is supposed to clear the entire game state, overriding the ways in which the usual save/load mechanics are subject to leakage via the rewind-immune memories of another character (a monster antagonist who, in the backstory, was accidentally resurrected by an infusion of the same reality-bending power of determination possessed by the player character, but lacking their own prior "SOUL" or its compassion). From there, it's possible to play for the "good" true-pacifist ending. Normally this ending has a scene at the end, either a cheerful group photo of the player character with the monsters who emigrated from the underworld, or a heartwarming domestic scene with the player character and their new monster adoptive mother. But after the genocide ending, both these scenes are irreversibly tainted; the player character's stoic expression is replaced by the merciless smile and red avid eyes of the ghost, and the faces in the group photo are crossed out in blood-red, implying that after the ending the formerly player-character hunted down and slew each of them.

There's a lot of ambiguity about what exactly is supposed to be what. I'm not sure the actual word "soul" is ever used with lowercase letters, but if "SOUL" is an acronym it's never explained. There's only a vague introduction of "your SOUL, the very culmination of your being", in reference to a little heart (a third the scale of the player character's body), which the player moves around to make selections from menus, and also to evade monsters' inherent magical attacks (and/or unwittingly hazardous magical self-expressions) in a "bullet-hell"-genre-inspired dodging arena that appears during enemy combat turns. (The last enemy on the genocide route has a number of fourth-wall-heavily-straining gimmicks, and one of them is that he uses the same attacks he uses inside the dodge arena against the the SOUL, while it's the player's turn, directly targeting the spaces next to the combat menu where the SOUL goes.) (In the much earlier game Afterlife, an afterlife city-management game by the unrelated company LucasArts, "SOUL" refers to "Stuff Of Unending Life", but that's probably not an intended reference.) It appears to have something to do with the player's access to the game world. In the sequel game it seems that the player's SOUL is in conflict with the new game's sometime player character, who at the end of chapter 1 beats themselves up to extract it and forces it into a golden cage; this might be because the SOUL is actually a moderating influence on them.

I wrote this before skimming the other comments, and it's weird that the other comments up as of this posting reference the idea of "player characters" in connection with incarnation, monsters, things not existing when a superior power isn't invested in them, the afterlife, and lingering ghosts.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's at least implied that the ghost is the one who has taken control and is conducting the negotiations. It's the same ghost that shows up in the later corrupted pacifist endings, after all.

This is a little bit of a sore spot for me because I come from a Christian background, where the idea of "action X constitutes knowing consent to be deceived into refusing rescue by grace and coming to fully deserve Hell, and trying to rationalize how this idea might be pathological and wrong also constitutes knowing consent to be deceived into refusing rescue by grace and coming to fully deserve Hell" behaved like a malignant cancer, that tried to attach itself to anything related to a different view of the supernatural than the Christian one. It's tied in with the whole binary internalization-projection self-hatred complex that critical theorists talk about: if you consent to the influence of anything your cultural authorities have labeled as the encroachments of the devil anywhere, it supposedly erodes your bright lines everywhere, and eventually blows up your whole moral practice and you start deserving the fates of the artificially-beat-upon outcast reprobates who are the projection-targets and internalization-sources for your repressed badself. For example, I think the mother of a family that was friends of ours banned her kids from playing any video games that had magic in them. This was not particularly uncommon in America thirty years ago.

However, of course that soreness wouldn't overrule hypothetical good epidemiological data about correlations between demonic-flavored derangements and playing the Undertale genocide route. And anecdotes are just a weaker form of epidemiological data, that are confounded by unknown latent cultural and individual degrees of propensity for data cherry-picking.

If Undertale were interpreted as a mundane artifact, there would be a quite coherent story in which Toby Fox was just trying to use the fourth wall as an additional medium of producing an artistic effect, presenting the player with the idea of potentially not having either moral or practical immunity from the consequences of using save/reload to try all the contingencies in the game to see what happens. This is literally the reasoning criticized by the last-ditch enemy on the genocide route: "and just because you 'can', you 'have to'". It's also the motive cited by the antagonist with the determination powers, for their descent into a horrific version of Groundhog Day (already undone before the game begins) and eventually ennui. The character who's the last-ditch enemy in the genocide route also, in the normal route, makes a number of pointed comments about whether you have a responsibility to go back and be more pacifist, since you're the only one who can control whether the underworld's problems get solved in a way that doesn't add to the pre-existing tally of tragedies. I suspect that the above story does, in fact, contain Toby Fox's actual conscious reasons for why to design the game that way.

That "moral of the story" is, of course, somewhat confounded by the fact that the final battle on the genocide route has arguably the best and certainly most iconic music in the game, though the final battle on the pacifist route has the second or third best.

It seems strange that "who or what inspired this game" should have bearing on how consent is construed here, if it wouldn't particularly be knowable to the person ostensibly doing the consenting.

comments.title: The Tree of Spirit
comments.content:
I would like to thank you for bringing the essays of John Gilbert to a wider audience. It is a real treasure! I am on Chapter 2 of “The Tree of Spirit” and it’s been an eye opener already. I realized how much work I have ahead of me if I want to gain even a speck of the vast knowledge of the occult. It is exiting! But it immediately brought some questions I need help with.

1. When J. Gilbert writes about subconscious mind represented by the High Priestess does he refer to the concept similar to your GSF concept of subjective mind? I may be mistaken, but if that is the case why did you choose to name it differently? Is it because the term subconsciousness has been overused by pop-psychology to the extent that it nearly lost all its original meaning? Or is it actually a different concept?

2. J. Gilbert states that when we decide to incarnate we choose the place, the time, the challenges, our responses to challenges, and the whole entourage beforehand. Does it mean that everything, and I mean EVERYTHING, already happened and known in advance? All we need is to play our part in a play already written. A true tragedy, in the sense that we know what will happen, we know the outcome, but we still play the part and we are not free to change our response because it was already chosen by us before we incarnated. We just go through the motions and our free will is an illusion. The only time we exercised free will was our will to incarnate.

3. But then how we can change ourselves (Strength) in the true meaning of the word change? It seems impossible because even that “change” was chosen in advance and is not a real change. How is it possible? Is it the Divine intervention we opened up to, The Chariot? Deus ex machina? Therefore, we did not know who we’d become and needed to incarnate to get the Divine intervention? So, the self-creation is actually self-co-creation, because without the Divine we’d be just “spinning the wheels” in a totally deterministic world, unable to evolve, to create, and to change.

I guess I worked myself into a corner now and cannot get out of it all by myself :)
Am I even on the right track?
Please help :)
Thank you.

replies.title: Re: The Tree of Spirit
replies.content:
1) I prefer the term "subjective" to "subconscious" because it's not necessarily below the surface of consciousness -- you experience the subjective mind directly in dreams, for example. I learned a lot from John but don't agree with him about everything.

2) No, not at all. It means we know what we're up against. It's as though you're playing Dungeons & Dragons and the DM lets you know in advance that you're up against trolls and an ice dragon, so your character chooses gear and weapons accordingly. That doesn't mean you know in advance everything that's going to happen and everything you will do in response -- among other things, you're not the only player character in the game!

3) Here again, you can always choose and change. The Divine is always present in you, and in everything else, so every creation is a co-creation with the Divine, because no one and nothing is ever without the Divine. Without the Divine, nothing would exist at all! So in one sense you can see life as a dance or a game the Divine plays with itself, becoming an infinity of beings and things in the process.

comments.title: Monsters and Bigfoot
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer,

As someone who wrote a book on monsters I thought I would throw some bigfoot questions at you.

First, does it exist?
Second, does it have a supernatural component or is this just a rare evolutionary cousin of ours?
Third, do you have any thoughts about Native American legends concerning big foot?

replies.title: Re: Monsters and Bigfoot
replies.content:
1) People certainly encounter creatures of that kind, and have done so for a very long time. There are medieval European accounts of "woodwoses," described as hairy humanoids who lived in the forest.

2) BHMs (big hairy monsters), to use the Fortean term for them, aren't all of the same kind. I'm pretty sure that the yetis of the Himalayas and the sasquatches of the Cascade Mountains are large bipedal apes, probably descended from the Gigantopithecus known to paleontologists, but some other BHMs act like preternatural beings -- for example, they vanish into thin air, leaving tracks that just stop in the middle of nowhere. So each sighting has to be assessed independently.

3) Always worth reading or (if you have the chance) listening to, but don't take them in the simpleminded white-guy way.

replies.title: Re: Monsters and Bigfoot
replies.content: 1. If they did indeed exist today, wouldn't we have found their physical remains by now? (Much less clearly caught them on camera)

replies.title: Re: Monsters and Bigfoot
replies.content:
This question gets asked fairly often by people who don't spend much time in the woods. It's uncommon to find physical remains of any animal in a wilderness setting, even if it's something really common, such as deer; it takes a very short time for carrion feeders of various kinds to reduce remains to invisibility. In this case, if the sasquatch exists as a biological animal, we're talking about small relict populations of a rare, nocturnal, and highly intelligent animal that avoids areas frequented by human beings.

Meanwhile people have found a great many tracks, feces, and hair samples. Scientists refuse to look at them. The unwritten law of the scientific community -- "no one but a tenured academic is allowed to discover anything" -- is just as powerful a force now as it was when continental drift was dismissed as crackpot pseudoscience.

replies.title: Re: Quan yin - Guanjin
replies.content:
Ewer might be the word you're looking for.

The Chinese dragon, actually called a loong or lung, is drastically different from the western dragon as it is associated with water rather than fire. It's strongly connected with chi, bringing good fortune, power, strength (provided you're worthy, of course). You often see the loong illustrated with a flaming pearl which represents wisdom and immortality. They are shapeshifters, shrinking down to tiny size or growing as big as the sky itself, even on occasion taking human form. While they are regarded as auspicious, if their ire should happen to be awakened, they can cause destructive floods and violent storms. (hmmm, we have been having an inordinate number of storms and floods lately, haven't we?....) Emperors often used its image for representing their power.

JLfromNH/Orange Waggish Gorgon

comments.title: Temple technology research update
comments.content:
Some time ago I wrote here about my ongoing research into the radio physics or electromagnetic features of the temple technology. I will be writing this up for you and your readers.

However, I've found the following absolutely fascinating things.

Firstly, the temple technology is most likely a dielectric resonator antennae, something known to radio enthusiasts and professionals for a long time. Dielectric antennae are not limited to the same fractions of wavelengths as metallic antennae, and they do not need paramagnetism to function (although this enhances the effect, or enables it without structure building, in the case of some standing stones). The temple of solomon and herods temple were made from calcite, which is diamagnetic and dielectric. Dielectric resonators have massive Q factors, meaning they store a lot of power in the form of standing waves in the stone.

The holy of holies in solomons temple has a resonant frequency of about 8hz. That's bang on one of the schuman resonances.

There's also a possibility that the gold lining on the calcite creates a piezoelectric effect, meaning that sound, in the way of a beat frequency with chanting, could excite the structure and enable additional effects. It could also work the other way, as a chamber enhancing meditation. Yes, this thing may have been a transceiver.

The actual mechanism by which fertility is enhanced is ultra weak photons,or non thermal photons. These flow out from the temple and excite plant roots.

Next step is for me to model the temple itself in software, which runs Maxwells equations over the 3 dimensional features of it.

Hope this helps.

comments.title: The inner dimmensions of language learning
comments.content:
After trying to learn several languages growing up both in and out of school never getting anywhere, and walking away discoursged and frustrated, I have spent most of my life telling myself I was just not capable of language learning. I remember the talk you gave that was eventually expanded into the title essay in A Magical Education when you mentioned learning a second language as an essential aspect of magical training just mentally checking that off as beyond my abilities and a handicap I’d have to cope with. Anyway, last year it clicked that every time I said “I just can’t learn languages” I was making a negative affirmation and actually ensuring I never would so I decided I’d try. Since then I’ve devoted a minimum of half an hour of every day or more to learning a second language (this time Cymraeg). Over the process of attaining this language I have made some observations that have led to questions for here. The first thing I’ve noticed since beginning is that this particular language has been easy for me in a way the others I’ve tried have not. It’s unfolded in natural and intuitive ways in my mind and half the time learning has felt more like remembering. The second thing I’ve noticed is what learning has felt like. The actual process of attaining language fluency so far has not felt like academic education, it has felt far more like magical training in an initiatory tradition. The types of changes that are happening in my mind have felt far more like the sorts of changes that unfold over time through daily ritual, divination, meditation, and pathworking through symbol systems than a purely mind level process of rote memorization.

All this has led to the question of what exactly is happening here on the inner planes?

What are you doing to yourself magically when you learn a second language? Is there a reason that it should feel so much like initiation?

Also, why do some people come more naturally to certain languages than others? I’ve talked to other language learners who have had that experience of struggling with several languages then finding something that just clicks into place.

replies.title: Re: The inner dimmensions of language learning
replies.content:
My personal hunch, and this is just my opinion, is that there may be a reincarnation component to it. I have had an affinity for Japan since I was 6 years old, and had the same kind of 'click' you did with Cymraeg, only for learning Japanese.

- Cicada Grove

replies.title: Re: The inner dimmensions of language learning
replies.content: Hmm! This is a fascinating question to which I don't have any cut and dried answers. I've learned several languages -- Russian in high school (all I remember these days are some swear words), Latin at university, French, Welsh, and Cornish each to some degree since then, and I'm currently working on Portuguese -- and I had roughly the same kind of experience with each one, which didn't seem much like initiation to me. So clearly there's a strong personal element here, too.

replies.title: Re: The inner dimmensions of language learning
replies.content:
“I had roughly the same kind of experience with each one?” What type of experience would you say that’s been for you?

It’s certainly been an odd process, and I’d have a hard time believing it’s a purely mundane process that doesn’t do -something- to the higher levels of self as much time and effort as it takes and as all consuming as it is. And there does seem to be some sort of magical component to language. You see things like the use of alphabets in actual magical systems, or emphasis on doing certain types of ritual in certain language to connect to channels of power, and it certainly looks like language has to reach deeper. At any rate, I’ve done puzzles, gotten academic degrees, and taken courses of magical training and the latter is what its felt the most like (right down to having weird portentious coincidences lining up one after another).

As Cicada Grove said, it certainly feels like there’s a karmic aspect to language… both in terms of which ones resonate with us and which ones we’re born into… It also feels like there’s some sort of linguistic egregore you’re tapping into with each language that differs from the others.

replies.title: Re: The inner dimmensions of language learning
replies.content: In my case, like learning anything else. I may simply not have much language karma -- if my memories are anything to go by, it's been five lives and most of a sixth since I last routinely spoke any language but English.

replies.title: Re: The inner dimmensions of language learning
replies.content:
I think of systems of things like astrological or mythological symbolism and egregores as only having the same kind of inherent connection to an underlying reality as language does. It's not no connection. Short words tend to be used for more common or important things, and grammar tends to make it possible to compose important meanings, and the words for species tend to put the boundaries in compatible places. But some other category boundaries aren't that shared, and of course the words themselves are in significant part random accidents of history.

It's sort of unsettling, seeing the tendency to fall into thinking of the forces or beings in a given system as being fundamental, while knowing that there are some ways in which this makes about as much sense as interpreting cosmic symbolism into the spatial relationships of letters on your keyboard (they had to fit together somewhere, otherwise you wouldn't have a keyboard!), and yet also suspecting that there are some benefits that can't be realized without letting part of your mind treat the system uncritically. I wonder if that's how a non-linguistic being would feel about having to adopt the meaning conventions of any language in particular. Then there's the thing reported by artists where learning to work within a system of apparently arbitrary constraints can help you recognize things you wouldn't have discovered in freeform.

comments.title: From the Dept. of Interesting Coinkydinks
comments.content:
ponerology: study of evil (fGreek poneros)
ponent (from Latin) In or of the West

hmmmm....I wonder.

Is there a Sanscrit or other Indic root to either of these words that might link them?

replies.title: Re: From the Dept. of Interesting Coinkydinks
replies.content:
Nope. Ponerology is from Greek poneros, from ponos "hard work, drudgery, pain," from Indo-European *(s)penh₁, "to weave or twist," the origin of the English word "spin," as in spinning yarn or thread.

Ponent ("where the sun goes down") is from Latin pono, "to place or set," which was *pozno in early Latin, in early Italic *po- + *sino' po- is "off, away," and *sino is "put, set, lay down," from Indo-European *tḱi-né-ti, a declension of *tḱey-, "to settle, to live."

(You can find all this in Wiktionary...)

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Hi JMG. Do you know why Christopher Columbus used the Templar cross on his ship? Was he a member of a surviving offshoot of the Knights Templar?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
We have no good contemporary images of Columbus's three ships, so nobody knows for sure what was or wasn't on the sails. Spanish ships generally had some form of cross on the sails to differentiate them from the pirate vessels of Muslim North Africa, but it's pure supposition to claim that Columbus had the Templar cross in particular.

Now if you asked why Portuguese vessels of the same era used the Templar cross, that's another matter; Portugal was one of two countries in Europe that refused to suppress the Templars when the Pope and Philip IV of France ganged up on them (the other was Scotland). The Templars in Portugal were renamed the Knights of Christ, and played an important role in Portugal's age of exploration -- the famous Prince Henry the Navigator was among other things Grand Master of the Knights of Christ. Portuguese ships used the cross of the Knights of Christ (aka the Templar cross) because of that. But that's Portugal, not Spain.

comments.title: Occultism's relative importance
comments.content:
Felicitous spring greetings to all :)

I've been pondering your posts on cycles of enchantment and a response to last magic Monday where you described the features of religion, occultism, and science. What has been formulating is a image of religion, occultism, and science as the three primary colors in Venn diagram form, with the places they overlap (the secondary colors,) begin defined as philosophy (occultism and science,) ethics (religion and science,) and psychology/mythology (occultism and religion.) I'm wondering if the state of disenchantment comes as those secondary pursuits try to stand on their own as if they aren't derived from the primary forms of knowing. This seems to be worth pondering, but it feels like perhaps a broken analogy/visualization.

Where is breaks for me is the role of occultism. Perhaps I'm skewed because of commitment to occult study, but it feels like through delving into occult practice and working on understanding one's own perception and intention and the power and limits of both, the practices of religion and science become more powerful and accurate so that occult practice is a balancing force. In contrast, from observing the lives of others, it appears that deep diving into religion or science, without a balancing occult practice (even mundane ones such as journaling) leads to imbalance and delusion.

One of my first thoughts as I read your most recent post on "The Destiny of Disenchantment," was that occult study provides a framework with which to avoid being thrown by tides of enchantment/disenchantment - a surfboard of sorts to ride the currents because you can actually see, and see through, those currents' effects on society's and one's own perceptions of the world. It also seems like the active parts of occult study are eminently useful, the creation and use of rituals is a way to maintain enchantment during low tide, and reflective arts like journaling and mediation are a way to maintain reason during high tide.

Am I giving occultism too much precedence?
Do you believe the original analogy of primary and secondary forms of knowing is worth pursuing or misguided in some way not obvious to me?
Do you view occultism as a mediating factor to the ebb and flow of enchantment; or is it perhaps tied to and reactive to those flows?

I appreciate this space and your thoughts.
Blessings to all who would receive them.

replies.title: Re: Occultism's relative importance
replies.content:
I see the Venn diagram a little differently:
[IMAGE:0]
Occultism is crucial because it deals with the realm between the material world that science investigates and the spiritual world that religion discusses. That middle realm is where enchantment and disenchantment take place, and it's precisely because our culture pretends that the middle realm doesn't exist that we're so clueless about enchantment and disenchantment -- and also why our sciences and our religions are so detached from one another.

replies.title: Re: Occultism's relative importance

Thank you. I am currently doing a second and close reading of the Jungian therapist James Hillman's essay The Feeling Function in the book Jung's Typology, it includes a discussion on the education of the feeling function. The distinction Hillman makes between feeling contents (anger, joy, etc) and the feeling function (the ability to handle feeling contents with skill and proportion) is useful.

The OSA so far seems to help very well with dealing with feeling contents from the past, at least it has for me, but I am less sure about whether the OSA educates the feeling function itself. Not least because it appears the OSA lessons in parts uses the thinking function to deal with emotions (i.e. by using thought to reframe an event to avoid negative emotions arising in the first place). My other doubts led to my questions in my initial post above.

It appears the idea of unconditional love comes up elsewhere, if I recall, the Bhagavad Gita also includes this idea in a way.

comments.title: Lifetime questions
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I have three questions, if I may:

1) I feel like I broke up with my 'One'/true love/soul mate/whatever-you-want-to-call-it 10 years ago. Despite the fact we did not part on bad terms, for a number of reasons, it's extremely unlikely we will ever even meet or speak again, let alone get back together. Is it likely we will meet again in another lifetime?
For some reason, I've been thinking of her a lot lately as well - could this be because she is thinking of me too?

2) On a related note, I always wanted to travel the world, but again, I don't think I'm going to be able to in this lifetime. Is that another one for "Next time around", or is it just something I'm unlikely to be interested in again in another life?

3) How does someone's astrologically-predicted death date work? Is it effectively the date they would die if they took no action (so for example, they may be astrologically predicted to die of a bacterial infection on a certain date, but if they accept/receive antibiotics, they won't die), or is it that no matter what action you take, something will kill you on that date regardless? (so for the above, you walk out of hospital, but get hit by a bus on the way home) And can you be astrologically-inclined to commit suicide on a certain date? (I do know that, as you've said before, "the stars incline, they do not compel" though)

Thanks.

replies.title: Re: Lifetime questions
replies.content:
1) If there's a significant tie of karma or destiny between you, yes, you'll meet in another life -- or possibly in this one.

2) You may be dimly remembering a life when you did just that. From the time of Magellan to the end of the 19th century, it was tolerably common for people to do just that.

3) The stars incline, they do not compel. What your chart shows is that you are at risk of death at a particular date -- not that you will certainly die on that day. Half the point of having your horoscope done is that you can look at the downsides and take action to keep from getting affected too badly by them.

replies.title: Re: Lifetime questions
replies.content:
On the topic of astrologically-predicted dates of death and "the stars incline but do not compel," there's an amusing story told about the Roman emperor Tiberius' court astrologer, Thrasyllus. Quoting Brennan's Hellenistic Astrology:

"The most famous legend [about Thrasyllus] involves his first meeting with Tiberius before he became the second Roman emperor. According to one version of the story, after Thrasyllus finished interpreting Tiberius' birth chart for the first time and predicted great things for him, Tiberius then turned the tables and asked Thrasyllus what his own chart said for the day. Apparently Tiberius was in the habit of having the astrologers he consulted with killed immediately after they were done reading his chart, by throwing them off a cliff. After doing some quick calculations Thrasyllus broke out into a cold sweat and exclaimed that he was in imminent danger. At this point, impressed by Thrasyllus' ability to determine the danger he was in simply from looking at his own chart, Tiberius made the astrologer one of his closest advisors."

comments.title: Lesser Pentagram Ritual
comments.content:
Good Evening Archdruid Emeritus,

Apologies if this has been covered before. I am a relatively new reader here.

I am a fairly new student practicing standard Golden Dawn magic. I am taking things slowly and sticking primarily with the LRP and the MP. I have studied materials on the Supreme Ritual of the Pentagram out of curiosity. I noticed that you can use the SRP to invoke or banish all the elements in one ritual with the proper pentagrams in the proper directions, the Spirit pentagrams, Enochian words etc.

This has led me to wonder, can you do that with the LRP? I have looked at several different sources for Golden Dawn rituals and they never seem to go into anymore detail than doing Earth invoking or banishing with the LRP. They don't seem to ever speak of using any element other than Earth either. I understand why Earth banishing is the "standard" form of the LBRP, and I'm not questioning that.

I know I am not ready for big invocations yet, but could I use the LIRP to invoke all, or even just one element, in similar fashion to the SIRP?

At the same time I am trying to be pretty "by the book" and am not looking to put my own spin on the GD system. If the LRP is restricted to single elements or even just Earth, why is that?

replies.title: Re: Lesser Pentagram Ritual
replies.content:
Good! Yes, you can use the LRP for the elements. The original GD didn't like to do this, because that doesn't include an explicit invocation of the element of spirit, but the invocation of the Divine Names and archangels takes care of that in my experience.

You can also use the LRH to invoke all seven planets, btw -- something to keep in mind when you get to that stage.

replies.title: Re: Lesser Pentagram Ritual
replies.content: Thank you so much for the response and for all the work that you do!

comments.title: Lies and Fraud
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
As everyone here is aware, we seem to be at a time of 'Peak Fraud' in the West, or at least close to it.
Do you think all this lying/cover-up/mendacious has an affect, on the so to speak, "collective mental realm which we are all moving about in", and that as it reaches its zenith and breaks apart, the liars and frauds who are creating it and those who (naively or not) buy in to it, will get splattered by all that piping hot raspberry jam (think donuts 😉) and those that don't go along with it, will for the most part escape intact from this mental realm corruption?
The consequences to follow are anyone's guess.
A bit of a rambling question, but I hope you get the gist of what I'm asking, with the emphasis on peak fraud of the mental realm

Regards, Helen in Oz

replies.title: Re: Lies and Fraud
replies.content:
Yes, very much so. Fraud is very dangerous stuff for the people who perpetrate it, because the lie and the truth are both patterns on the mental plane and they cancel each other out, so compulsive liars end up losing a chunk of mental plane capacity. You can give yourself a life with severe developmental disabilities if you do this enthusiastically enough, or even drop out of human incarnation altogether and have to work your way back up.

You're correct, also, that it's the ones who manufacture and spread the lies who come off worst. Those who believe them naively get the karma of being naive and clueless, which is no fun but is a lot less burdensome; those who reject one set of lies but proceed to embrace and spread another -- well, karma is infinitely flexible. Personal honesty and an attitude of true skepticism (not the false skepticism of those who are only skeptical of things that contradict their pet dogmas) tends to have the best results.

replies.title: Re: Lies and Fraud
replies.content: Thank you!
